tldr: flagd is a self-hosted OpenFeature-compliant flag daemon configured via files; Bucket is a hosted feature management SaaS with a polished dashboard. Migration trades operational ownership for a managed product and means re-creating flags, targeting, and SDK wiring inside Bucket.
why_switch: Stop running and on-calling a self-hosted flag daemon
Get a polished UI for non-engineers to manage flags
Built-in feature-management workflows beyond raw evaluation
Reduce operational scope and free up platform engineering time
Centralize flag state instead of distributing config files
what_to_export: Targeting rules and segment logic per flag
Flag descriptions, owners, and ticket references
Custom evaluator extensions or sync providers in use
OpenTelemetry trace context fields you depend on
export_method: flagd's source of truth is its flag configuration files (JSON or YAML), typically loaded via file, HTTP, gRPC, or Kubernetes sync providers. Collect every config file across every environment from your repo or sync source. Treat these files as the export — they already contain flags, variants, defaults, and targeting rules in a portable format. Inventory any custom sync providers, evaluator overrides, or operator setups so they can be replaced or retired. Snapshot the full set of files at the cutover commit so you can diff against the migrated state in Bucket and confirm parity flag-by-flag.
import_method: Bucket has no native flagd importer, so re-create flags through its UI or API. For each flagd flag, create a corresponding feature in Bucket with the same key, default value, and variant set. Translate flagd targeting rules into Bucket's segmentation model — user attributes, company attributes, and rollout percentages. Replace the flagd OpenFeature provider in your services with Bucket's SDK or, if you want to keep OpenFeature, with a Bucket OpenFeature provider where one is available. Update identify calls to pass the user/company context Bucket expects. Validate each flag in a staging environment before swapping production traffic, and keep flagd running in parallel until parity is confirmed. Decommission flagd's daemon, sync providers, and any custom operators only after a full bake period.

concept_mapping.note: Re-create via UI or API; key naming should match to minimize code churn
Logical operators differ; re-test edge cases

concept_mapping.target: Bucket SDK or Bucket OpenFeature provider

concept_mapping.note: Telemetry shape will change; instrument accordingly

gotchas: Going from self-hosted to SaaS means user data leaves your perimeter — review privacy and compliance posture before cutover
JsonLogic-style targeting rules in flagd may not map 1:1 to Bucket's rule engine; validate complex predicates carefully
Bucket pricing scales with usage; high-QPS workloads can be materially more expensive than free flagd
OpenTelemetry tracing of every evaluation goes away unless Bucket exposes equivalent telemetry
Custom sync providers and operator setups for flagd have no SaaS equivalent and must be retired
Local-only zero-latency evaluation may be replaced by network calls; benchmark hot paths

email.subject: Migrating feature flags from self-hosted flagd to Bucket
email.body:
Hi team,

We're planning to move our feature flagging from self-hosted flagd to Bucket, a managed feature-management platform, over the next 14 days. The aim is a friendlier UI for non-engineers and less infrastructure for us to operate.

What changes for you:
- Flag definitions will move out of our Git-managed config files into the Bucket dashboard.
- Services will swap the flagd OpenFeature provider for Bucket's SDK (or its OpenFeature provider where applicable).
- Targeting rules will be re-implemented in Bucket and validated before switchover.

We'll run both systems in parallel during the overlap and decommission flagd only after parity is confirmed. Please flag any rule or workflow in flagd you depend on so we can verify it survives the migration.

Thanks,
Your customer
